questions.questionText: The most common presentation in labor is:
questions.optionB: Vertex/cephalic presentation with left occipitoanterior (LOA) position (most common and most favorable)
questions.explanation: Vertex presentation: 96% of all deliveries. Most common position: left occiput anterior (LOA). Mechanism of labor (cardinal movements): (1) Engagement, (2) Descent, (3) Flexion, (4) Internal rotation (AP diameter of pelvis at outlet), (5) Extension (head delivers), (6) External rotation (restitution), (7) Expulsion (shoulders and body). Breech: 3-4%. Face, brow, shoulder: rare. Reference: DC Dutta — Normal Labor.

questions.questionText: Active management of third stage of labor (AMTSL) includes:
questions.optionB: Uterotonic (oxytocin 10 IU IM) immediately after delivery, controlled cord traction, and uterine massage
questions.explanation: AMTSL: prevents postpartum hemorrhage (leading cause of maternal death). Components: (1) Uterotonic within 1 min of delivery (oxytocin 10 IU IM — drug of choice, or ergometrine, or misoprostol), (2) Controlled cord traction (Brandt-Andrews maneuver — suprapubic counter-pressure + gentle cord traction), (3) Uterine massage after placental delivery. Reduces PPH by 60-70%. Third stage: from delivery of baby to delivery of placenta + membranes (normally <30 min). Reference: DC Dutta — AMTSL.

questions.questionText: The most common indication for cesarean section worldwide is:
questions.optionB: Previous cesarean section (repeat CS) — followed by non-progress of labor/dystocia
questions.explanation: Most common indication for CS: previous CS (repeat CS). Other common indications: non-progress of labor/dystocia (most common primary indication), fetal distress, cephalopelvic disproportion, malpresentation (breech, transverse), placenta previa (major), cord prolapse, multiple pregnancy complications. VBAC (vaginal birth after cesarean): can be attempted in select cases (one previous lower segment CS, no contraindication). Classical CS scar: contraindication to VBAC (risk of rupture). Reference: DC Dutta — Cesarean Section.
